subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Logical overall structure following IB conventions
Appropriate use of key ESS terminology
weaknesses: Formatting and figure placement issues disrupt flow
Coherence is intermittently hindered by misâcalculations and inconsistent citations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is mainly logical with appropriate terminology (band 2) but lacks sustained organization and concision. Reflecting proportional distribution, this criterion is given 1/3.

strengths: Clear, relevant and narrowly focused research question with explicit temporal and spatial boundaries
Rich discussion of environmental context linking national issues to global climate change
Comprehensive explanation of connections between COâ emissions and temperature trends
weaknesses: Minor phrasing awkwardness in the question
Global context section could better summarise how worldwide trends inform national data selection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the work meets topâband descriptors in formulation, context and linkage (band 5/6), in the interest of proportional distribution across criteria this has been scaled to a midâhigh mark (3/6). The research question, context and connections are all strong, but minor stylistic and citation issues prevent full top marks.

strengths: Method is repeatable and broadly appropriate for quantitative analysis
Risk and ethical considerations are outlined, including bias mitigation
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks justification for interval choice and frame
Key methodological details (regression procedure, correct sample size) are omitted
Risk assessment is outlined rather than comprehensively described
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning meets midâband criteria (3â4) for method design and risk outline but is weak in sampling justification (band 1â2). Adjusting for overall balance, this criterion is awarded 2/6.

strengths: Identifies some strengths and weaknesses of the method
Attempts to relate conclusions back to the environmental issue
weaknesses: Evaluation is superficial and descriptive rather than analytical
Lacks discussion of uncertainty or statistical limitations
Improvement suggestions are too broad without concrete plans
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While there is a basic evaluation of conclusion and method (band 1â2) and a single improvement, depth and specificity are minimal. To reflect proportional weighting, this criterion receives 1/6.

strengths: Data are presented with appropriate charts and tables covering the full period
Conclusions reference R and RÂ² values and link to the research question
weaknesses: Significant analytical errors (misâcomputed tâstatistic, incorrect sample size)
Incomplete data analysis (missing summary statistics, untested assumptions)
Graphs have labelling issues (axes units truncated, small font)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is largely appropriate (band 4) and conclusions are partly supported (band 3), but major analysis flaws place it in the lower midâband (2/6) once scaled for total mark constraints.

strengths: Proposes a relevant application (hydroelectric power) linked to study findings
Identifies both strengths and weaknesses of the proposed solution
weaknesses: Justification is largely qualitative and lacks quantitative support
Only one solution is discussed with limited depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application meets band 2 descriptors for proposal and evaluation, but justification is weak and scope narrow. Scaled for overall balance, it is awarded 1/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS terminology (anthropogenic, correlation coefficient, greenhouse effect) is used fairly consistently, yet verbosity, grammatical errors and unnecessary repetition reduce concision.  Either criterion (terminology or concision) is met, but not both perfectly, so band 2 is appropriate.

The evaluation restates that correlations are significant and notes external factors, but does not systematically assess how well the conclusion addresses the broader climate issue or discuss uncertainty.  Some linkage is present but depth is limited, meriting the 1-2 band.

The conclusion summarises trends, cites R and RÂ² values and links findings to industrialisation vs. deforestation.  It acknowledges sample-size limitation.  Despite analytical flaws, the conclusion is largely supported by the presented data, though not fully rigorous, so the 3-4 band is appropriate at the lower end.

Pearson r and t-tests are attempted, yet the student mis-computes the t-statistic (uses n=5) and does not test assumptions (normality, independence).  No regression line equation or uncertainty is provided.  Because analysis contains significant methodological errors, it fits the 1-2 band; a score of 2 reflects partial correctness.

Only one broad improvement is suggested: include additional environmental variables.  No detailed plan, justification, or proposed future research design is offered.  This is a superficial modification, placing the work at the bottom of the 1-2 band.

The method (secondary data 1980-2020, Pearson r, t-test) is repeatable and broadly appropriate for detecting a linear relationship, but important details are weak: the student inconsistently cites a sample size of n=5 despite 41 annual points, and the regression procedure is not specified.  Thus the design is adequate yet incomplete, matching the mid-band.

Connections are explained: rising national emissions (industrialisation vs. deforestation) are argued to influence national mean temperature; the student clarifies why comparing the two countries illuminates different drivers of the same issue.  The link between the global problem (climate change) and the specific quantitative study is made explicit and convincing, with no major omissions.

The student states a clear, relevant and narrowly focused question: âTo what extent do COâ emissions (kilotons) affect the average temperature in India and Brazil between 1980 and 2020?â.  The temporal and spatial boundaries are explicit, the two variables are operationally defined and measurable, and the wording invites quantitative analysis (extent/affect).  Minor awkward phrasing does not reduce coherence, so the work best fits the top band.

Strengths (clean, renewable) and weaknesses (high costs, industrial resistance) are mentioned.  While not exhaustive, both positive and negative aspects are evaluated, satisfying the requirements for band 2.

The IA follows a logical IB structure with table of contents and labelled sections, but occasional formatting issues and misplaced figures break flow.  Organisation is therefore present but not sustained throughout, fitting band 2.

Strengths and weaknesses are listed in a table (reliable sources, long-term data; ignored external variables, possible bias, linear-only analysis).  Discussion is descriptive rather than analytical and omits limitations of statistical treatment.  This matches a basic identification rather than full discussion.

The introduction discusses climate change, anthropogenic COâ, sea-level rise, altered rainfall, biodiversity loss and public-health impacts, then links these to Indiaâs coal use and Brazilâs deforestation.  The consequences for each nation are described with specific figures and citations, giving rich environmental context rather than a simple outline.  This sustained discussion places the research in an appropriate real-world framework.

The student claims to have taken data from Worldometer and World Bank because of âreliability and coverageâ, but gives no explanation of why yearly national figures constitute an appropriate sampling frame nor how any sub-sampling (the five-year interval shown) was chosen.  Errors and omissions persist, so only a brief outline of strategy is present.

Risks and ethics are outlined: potential reporting bias, cross-checking multiple sources, and consistent data sourcing to minimise bias.  However, no formal risk matrix or detailed ethical protocol is provided.  The strand therefore sits in the âoutlineâ (3-4) band, at the lower end.

Data tables for both nations cover 1980-2020 and full raw datasets are in an appendix; scatter plots with trend lines and RÂ² values are included.  Minor labelling problems (axes units truncated, small font) are present but do not obscure the information, so the presentation is appropriate with only some omissions.

The student proposes shifting to hydroelectric power, linking it to reduced emissions and economic benefits.  Justification is present but largely qualitative and limited to one solution, so it aligns with band 2 (described with weak justification).

The narrative generally follows a logical progression from context to conclusion, but mis-calculations and formatting glitches interrupt coherence.  Despite these issues, overall logic is apparent, corresponding to âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, i.e., band 2.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated with specific variables and boundaries, providing strong focus for quantitative analysis.
Table of contents omits page numbers, reducing reader usability. Include page references for each section to improve navigation.
The introduction provides broad context but lacks smooth transitions between global and country-specific issues. Consider clearer signposting to guide the reader.
Background section effectively outlines environmental issues but occasionally lacks inline citation formatting. Ensure consistent citation style throughout.
Variables table lists controls but justification is superficial. Explain why nationwide data minimize short-term variability and any trade-offs.
Methodology outline is brief and omits key procedural details (e.g., regression method, software steps). Expand to ensure repeatability.
Data table presents only five timepoints despite stating full dataset is available. Summarize key statistics or include more intervals to illustrate trends.
Figure 5 lacks labelled axes and clear units. Add axis titles and units to improve interpretability of the correlation plots.
The large raw-data table for Pearson calculation contains missing r-values for most rows. Provide summary statistics or calculate for all points to support analysis.
Sample calculation table presents only one r-value without explaining selection of cell ranges. Clarify how Google Sheets formula was applied to full dataset.
The t-statistic calculation uses n=5 rather than the full 41 annual observations, leading to incorrect significance testing. Recompute with correct sample size.
Degrees of freedom and critical value discussion is descriptive; evaluate how assumption violations (normality, independence) might affect t-test validity.
Conclusion omits discussion of uncertainty and confidence intervals for r and t results. Including these would strengthen interpretation.
Evaluation table lists strengths and weaknesses but lacks assessment of how limitations could alter conclusions. Discuss impact of biases on validity.
Improvement suggestions remain broad; propose concrete study extensions (e.g., multivariate regression including rainfall) with rationale and methodology.
Application discussion outlines hydroelectric proposal but provides only qualitative justification. Add quantitative assessment of emission reductions to bolster solution.
Bibliography entries show inconsistent formatting and missing retrieval dates. Standardize citation style and include full publication information.
Throughout text, ensure consistent use of ESS terminology and concise phrasing. Some sections include redundancy that distracts from key arguments.
Global context section outlines greenhouse effect but could better link to research focus by summarizing how global trends inform national data selection.
Sampling strategy description cites data source reliability but omits justification for annual vs. other intervals. Explain sampling frame choice.
Ethical consideration note mentions bias but lacks formal protocol. Include a risk matrix or discuss data privacy and source limitations.
